'webTarget' was derived from 'HttpClient' -- see 'Server.getTarget(HttpClient client)'. The idea was to build 'HttpClient' just once for each remote 'Server' instance, and then build on top of that to construct the specific query. I didn't see an efficient way to do that with the current API.

There is a similar capability in class 'Transaction' in the m2 model, and a slightly different capability in 'PolicyContainer' and 'PolicySession' in 'core'. It might be a good idea to try to come up with something generic that could be used everywhere. It gets a bit challenging to do this cleanly, because these are template methods.

looks like this and a number of the other statics (e.g., timeToLive) could be moved to a "Context" object that is passed to the constructor.  That would potentially make it much easier to junit test a Bucket by giving it a new Context.

I'm noticing lots of code that seems to be similar for both owner, primary, and secondary.  Seems like this would imply the need for some additional abstraction.  If that were done, the owner, primary, and secondary objects could be placed into an ArrayList.  That would still provide direct indexing via get/put, but would also be convenient for iterating.  Once that's done, operations could be performed simply by iterating over each item in the list.

Even if abstraction cannot be done, it still might make sense to put them all into an array/ArrayList so that the same method could be used to manipulate primary and secondary data; just pass a different index to the method.

There are subtle differences between primary and secondary backup, and more significant differences between either of these and owner. My gut feeling is that accessing all of these through an array would lead to a few opportunities for reuse, but many more opportunities for confusion.

A less disruptive approach would be to define a method 'Server getBucketServer(int index)' and 'void setBucketServer(Server server, int index)', where index has the range 0-2, and these could be used any place where all three are treated the same (I don't know exactly where at this point).

It would mean that the data is corrupted -- one of those "this should never happen" things, like 'IOException'. If it does happen, the caller logs an error message, and the update operation is aborted.
Most of 'copyData' runs in the 'MainLoop' thread, which is also the only thread where bucket updates occur.

Actually, it looks like the callback would be missed if an exception occurs -- good catch!

I think the best fix would be to add a new method to interface 'Server.PostResponse' that is called if an exception occurs, and give it a default implementation that generates an appropriate 'Response' object, and calls 'response(Response)'. That way, callers who want to explicitly see exceptions can, and those that just want the failure response don't need to do anything different.

The actual updates only occur in the 'MainLoop' thread, so there shouldn't be any synchronization issues. I suppose it is possible that an automatic rebalance and a rebalance from a REST command could be interleaved, but the end result would be two rebalances in quick succession -- there shouldn't be any corruption, because the 'Rebalance.copyData()' and update operations are both "atomic", as both run in the MainLoop thread.
The key needs to be that of an existing active host, other than the current host.

Is there a benefit to making it 'final'? At present, we aren't reassigning this anywhere, but you could envision an algorithm where you end up generating a new TreeSet, rather than incrementally updating 'buckets'.
Updates to 'owner', 'primaryBackup', and 'secondaryBackup' always occur within the 'MainLoop' thread.

It shouldn't be -- the 'testServers' table should be an atomic snapshot of the 'Servers' table. As long as the 'Servers' table isn't corrupted, the 'testServers' table won't be either. 

If the 'Servers' table somehow did get corrupted, a 'null' return from 'get' would be interpreted as an unassigned 'owner' (or 'primaryBackup' or 'secondaryBackup'), and the rebalance algorithm would assign one.

The 'bucket.setOwner(ts)' operation (below) removes the bucket from it's current "Server" (which is nullServer), and adds it to the newly-assigned server 'ts'. This means that 'nullServer.buckets' is being changed as we iterate through the loop, which would result in a 'ConcurrentModificationException', unless we iterate over a copy.

Yes - 'setOwner' updates the old and new owner's bucket set ('setPrimaryBackup' and 'setSecondaryBackup' behave in a similar manner). At this point in the code, 'last' has at least two more buckets than 'first', so one bucket is moved from 'last' to 'first' to bring them closer in balance.

'/cmd/dumpBuckets' is a test/debugging command. It is most often invoked by a human running 'curl', but should still display correctly if you use a browser. I find it more readable than HTML or JSON (with or without pretty-printing).

'register' is only called at initialization time -- 'backupList' doesn't change after that point.

If a bucket migrates from one server to another, 'Backup.generate(int bucketNumber)' is called on every entry on the list, the resulting objects are serialized, sent to the new host, deserialized, and 'Restore.restore(int bucketNumber)' is called for each entry on the destination host.

This is not the overall wait time -- there is a polling loop (lines 2193-2198) that checks the state at 1 second intervals. An actual error time-out occurs when 'endTime' is reached, which is typically something like 30 seconds after start.

No, this is correct -- if 'this.data != null', we have previously received backup data, and are presumably acting on it. I suppose we could consider logging an error message if we get redundant backup data, although it may be that retransmissions are not unusual -- I wrote this stuff back in 2017, and I don't remember all of the details.

OK - this is one of those fuzzy 2017 memories I alluded to earlier :-)

When bucket migration is occurring, we want to queue incoming messages until all of the associated data has been restored, and then process messages in order, continuing to queue them until we catch up.

I believe there was a situation where the 'process()' method associated with incoming UEB/DMAAP messages acted just like a new incoming message, and it wound up back in the queue. I will need to do some more digging to see what this was about, and verify that it was fixed.
At this point, I don't know what would happen if a bucket is moved from server 1 to server 2 and then to server 3 in quick succession. It seems safer to just discard the data, and log an error.
seems like a perfect opportunity for some object abstraction, where the "subtle differences" would be captured in subclasses
can't just generate a random number that's one less than the number of servers?  if count=current server then set count=last server

the benefit is that it prevents something else from changing it's value.  If the code changes later so that it intentionally changes its value, then "final" can be removed at that time.  Basically, it provides a safety check for things you don't expect to change.  I don't frequently use it within the body of a method, but I often use it in class definitions, if it's something that shouldn't be changed
got it - didn't realize the setOwner() might change it.  Perhaps add a comment to that effect so folks in the future don't wonder the same thing
I assume there won't be many backup items?  Otherwise, could use a Set, which is still more efficient than a List.
Oh, duh, I was reading it as "data == null"
The servers are in a TreeMap -- it seems that some kind of stepping is required in order to get a random distribution. This is in a REST command used for testing, so performance isn't an issue.

StandardCoder (line 59) is being used to convert a JSON message to a JsonObject, which is then used to locate the keyword that ultimately determines where the message is routed. See the 'beforeOffer' method on line 275.

this stuff below could be reused in other places (e.g., StandardCoderObject) if it were a full-fledged object, without the static data/methods

Let's just stick with StandardCoderObject; it does the path loop already, just need to deal with the conversion function.  You might have a better idea, but here are the three that I see:
1) server pool:
     copies path, stripping the ":xxx" suffix from the last component
     invokes object.getString(copied path)
     invokes the conversion function with the result
2) StandardCoderObject:
     add a new method getConvertedString(converter, path)
         converter takes the value and the function name (i.e., the suffix of the last
         path component) and returns the converted value
   server pool:
     invokes object.getConvertedString(converter, path)
3) StandardCoderObject:
     modify getString(path) to strip any ":xxx" suffix from the last component
   server pool:
     invokes object.getString(path)
     invokes the conversion function on the result

I like #1, because it seems like this conversion stuff is ancillary to StandardCoderObject.  On the other hand, I could see that the conversion stuff might be useful, so #2 may actually be the better answer.  I can also do #3, though that seems the least favorable of these options.

Api interfaces have the OrderedService* portion separated with *Api and *Constants.   See other projects in this repo for reference.   It would be best to be consistent across all projects.

In general prefix with "server.pool", to follow convention we are trying to follow in other modules to be representative that is exclusive to server-pooling.  "server" prefix is too generic.

In general, fyi, if this is for a standalone jax-rs servlet, for example for protocol communication between components, it does not need to be in this package.   If this class is for diagnostics and "user" interaction, not machine to machine protocols, this should be in this package to integrate cleanly under telemetry.  Not sure here, if for "user" consumption, it should integrate under telemetry under "policy/pdp/engine" root context paths, as the other features do.   So, the comment here, for user commands (and only user commands), place url access under "policy/pdp/engine/.." similar to all the other existing telemetry apis.    For machine to machine communication, they can be in its own http-server object, whereever you want to place them under a different package.

This 'await' is completely unnecessary -- the 'KieSession' should exist by the time the @BeforeClass method completes.
This 'await' is completely unnecessary -- the 'KieSession' should exist by the time the @BeforeClass method completes.

This isn't going to do what you want -- the 'KieSession' already exists by the time the @BeforeClass method completes. However, there may be a delay before the object insert completes -- that is the condition you need to wait for.

This version also needs to match the value in the properties file -- this is why the tests are failing. I'm not sure why it was changed, but I think it would be better to use a SNAPSHOT version in any case.
